--- index.tsx ---
import { StrictMode } from 'react'
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'

import App from './App'
import * as serviceWorker from './serviceWorker'

const container = document.querySelector('#root') as HTMLElement
const root = createRoot(container)

root.render(
  <StrictMode>
    <App />
  </StrictMode>
)

// If you want your app to work offline and load faster, you can change
// unregister() to register() below. Note this comes with some pitfalls.
// Learn more about service workers: https://bit.ly/CRA-PWA
serviceWorker.unregister()

--- App.tsx ---
import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

--- Routes.tsx ---
import { useLayoutEffect } from 'react'
import { Routes, Route, useLocation } from 'react-router-dom'

import { CategoryListPage } from './pages/CategoryListPage'
import { CategoryDetailPage } from './pages/CategoryDetailPage'
import { RestaurantDetailPage } from './pages/RestaurantDetailPage'
import { HomePage } from './pages/HomePage'
import { CheckoutPage } from './pages/CheckoutPage'
import { SuccessPage } from './pages/SuccessPage'

export const AppRoutes: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<unknown>> = ({ children }) => {
  const location = useLocation()
  // Scroll to top when a path changes
  useLayoutEffect(() => {
    window.scrollTo(0, 0)
  }, [location.pathname])

  return (
    <>
      <div id="modal" />
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/categories" element={<CategoryListPage />} />
        <Route path="/categories/:id" element={<CategoryDetailPage />} />
        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />
        <Route path="/checkout" element={<CheckoutPage />} />
        <Route path="/success" element={<SuccessPage />} />
        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
        {children}
      </Routes>
    </>
  )
}

transcript.text: I’ve got the runtime model now: `BrowserRouter -> Redux store -> ThemeProvider -> GlobalStyle`, global CSS comes from styled-components plus the carousel/skeleton packages, the only portal root is `#modal`, and the only live data surface is the restaurants endpoint with `id`/`category` query variants. I’m moving into the shared Storybook environment now so the stories can stay close to real usage instead of carrying their own mocks or wrappers.

Plan for the Storybook pass:

1. Rebuild the shared preview so it matches the real app shell.
   I’m going to wrap stories in `BrowserRouter`, a fresh Redux store, `ThemeProvider`, and `GlobalStyle`, seed the persisted dark-mode key, reset body scroll/classes between stories, and let stories pass only route/store parameters instead of custom harness code.

2. Add the shared side-effect support once.
   I’ll wire `msw-storybook-addon`, serve `public`, create a single restaurants handler that covers list, `?category=...`, and `?id=...`, and add `.storybook/preview-body.html` with the `#modal` root so modal stories use the real portal target.

3. Add colocated stories in meaningful layers.
   I’m targeting reusable components first, then store/router-backed components, then pages: things like `Category`, `RestaurantCard`, `Header`, `HomePage`, `CategoryListPage`, `CategoryDetailPage`, `RestaurantDetailPage`, `CheckoutPage`, and `SuccessPage`, using JSX patterns copied from the app.

4. Verify each story file as it lands.
   After each new story file I’ll run `npx vitest --project storybook <that-file>` and fix anything before adding the next one, then finish with `npx tsc --noEmit`.

I’m editing the shared Storybook setup first because that’s the foundation every story depends on. This includes the preview decorators, the global MSW handlers, the static `public` serving, and the modal root in `preview-body.html`.

transcript.text: The category-detail stories uncovered a real mismatch in the shared mock layer: the page routes use the slug `comfort-food`, while the restaurant stub data stores `comfort food`. I’m fixing that in the global MSW handler so the mock matches the route behavior everywhere, not just in one story.

I’m on the most integration-heavy page now. `RestaurantDetailPage` touches routed params, MSW data, Redux cart state, and the modal portal, so getting these stories green will confirm the preview setup is doing the real work rather than the stories compensating for missing runtime pieces.

transcript.text:
**What Changed**

Storybook now boots with a faithful app runtime in [.storybook/preview.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/preview.tsx): `BrowserRouter`, a fresh Redux store per story, the real `useDarkMode` theme flow, `ThemeProvider`, and `GlobalStyle`. It also seeds route/localStorage state from `parameters.app`, so routed pages and dark mode work without story-local harnesses.

Global data mocking lives in [.storybook/msw-handlers.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts), with `msw-storybook-addon` added in [package.json](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/package.json). I also added the modal portal root in [.storybook/preview-body.html](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/preview-body.html), served `public` from [.storybook/main.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/main.ts), and prebundled the app’s Storybook-heavy deps in [vite.config.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/vite.config.ts) to stop browser-test reload flakiness.

--- index.tsx ---
import { StrictMode } from 'react'
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'

import App from './App'
import * as serviceWorker from './serviceWorker'

const container = document.querySelector('#root') as HTMLElement
const root = createRoot(container)

root.render(
  <StrictMode>
    <App />
  </StrictMode>
)

// If you want your app to work offline and load faster, you can change
// unregister() to register() below. Note this comes with some pitfalls.
// Learn more about service workers: https://bit.ly/CRA-PWA
serviceWorker.unregister()

--- App.tsx ---
import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

--- Routes.tsx ---
import { useLayoutEffect } from 'react'
import { Routes, Route, useLocation } from 'react-router-dom'

import { CategoryListPage } from './pages/CategoryListPage'
import { CategoryDetailPage } from './pages/CategoryDetailPage'
import { RestaurantDetailPage } from './pages/RestaurantDetailPage'
import { HomePage } from './pages/HomePage'
import { CheckoutPage } from './pages/CheckoutPage'
import { SuccessPage } from './pages/SuccessPage'

export const AppRoutes: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<unknown>> = ({ children }) => {
  const location = useLocation()
  // Scroll to top when a path changes
  useLayoutEffect(() => {
    window.scrollTo(0, 0)
  }, [location.pathname])

  return (
    <>
      <div id="modal" />
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/categories" element={<CategoryListPage />} />
        <Route path="/categories/:id" element={<CategoryDetailPage />} />
        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />
        <Route path="/checkout" element={<CheckoutPage />} />
        <Route path="/success" element={<SuccessPage />} />
        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
        {children}
      </Routes>
    </>
  )
}

I’m reading the shell components and current Storybook config next so we can wire one faithful global setup instead of compensating inside stories.

content.text: I’ve got the runtime model now: `BrowserRouter -> Redux store -> ThemeProvider -> GlobalStyle`, global CSS comes from styled-components plus the carousel/skeleton packages, the only portal root is `#modal`, and the only live data surface is the restaurants endpoint with `id`/`category` query variants. I’m moving into the shared Storybook environment now so the stories can stay close to real usage instead of carrying their own mocks or wrappers.

Plan for the Storybook pass:

1. Rebuild the shared preview so it matches the real app shell.
   I’m going to wrap stories in `BrowserRouter`, a fresh Redux store, `ThemeProvider`, and `GlobalStyle`, seed the persisted dark-mode key, reset body scroll/classes between stories, and let stories pass only route/store parameters instead of custom harness code.

2. Add the shared side-effect support once.
   I’ll wire `msw-storybook-addon`, serve `public`, create a single restaurants handler that covers list, `?category=...`, and `?id=...`, and add `.storybook/preview-body.html` with the `#modal` root so modal stories use the real portal target.

3. Add colocated stories in meaningful layers.
   I’m targeting reusable components first, then store/router-backed components, then pages: things like `Category`, `RestaurantCard`, `Header`, `HomePage`, `CategoryListPage`, `CategoryDetailPage`, `RestaurantDetailPage`, `CheckoutPage`, and `SuccessPage`, using JSX patterns copied from the app.

4. Verify each story file as it lands.
   After each new story file I’ll run `npx vitest --project storybook <that-file>` and fix anything before adding the next one, then finish with `npx tsc --noEmit`.

I’m editing the shared Storybook setup first because that’s the foundation every story depends on. This includes the preview decorators, the global MSW handlers, the static `public` serving, and the modal root in `preview-body.html`.

content.text: Vitest caught a real setup issue rather than a story bug: Vite re-optimized a newly encountered dependency (`react-loading-skeleton`) during the browser run, which invalidated the Storybook test session. I’m fixing that at the Vite/Vitest layer so the browser-story tests stay stable as we add more files.
I’m making a small Vite config tweak before rerunning the same story file. This keeps the browser test runner from hot-reloading mid-test when Storybook first encounters carousel/skeleton packages.

content.text: The failing category-detail stories exposed the next missing piece in the preview: routed pages that read `useParams` need a matching route wrapper, not just a `BrowserRouter`. I’m adding that to the shared preview so parameterized pages work the same way without inventing wrappers inside each story.

content.text: The category-detail stories uncovered a real mismatch in the shared mock layer: the page routes use the slug `comfort-food`, while the restaurant stub data stores `comfort food`. I’m fixing that in the global MSW handler so the mock matches the route behavior everywhere, not just in one story.

File changes:
- update /Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts

I’m on the most integration-heavy page now. `RestaurantDetailPage` touches routed params, MSW data, Redux cart state, and the modal portal, so getting these stories green will confirm the preview setup is doing the real work rather than the stories compensating for missing runtime pieces.

content.text: The restaurant-detail stories are rendering the right states now, and the remaining failures are down to over-specific assertions inside animated/duplicated UI. I’m tightening those checks so they prove the modal and 404 state work without depending on brittle text-node visibility edge cases.

I’m tightening those types now; the runtime behavior is already good.

content.text:
**What Changed**

Storybook now boots with a faithful app runtime in [.storybook/preview.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/preview.tsx): `BrowserRouter`, a fresh Redux store per story, the real `useDarkMode` theme flow, `ThemeProvider`, and `GlobalStyle`. It also seeds route/localStorage state from `parameters.app`, so routed pages and dark mode work without story-local harnesses.

Global data mocking lives in [.storybook/msw-handlers.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts), with `msw-storybook-addon` added in [package.json](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/package.json). I also added the modal portal root in [.storybook/preview-body.html](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/preview-body.html), served `public` from [.storybook/main.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/.storybook/main.ts), and prebundled the app’s Storybook-heavy deps in [vite.config.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-10T17-02-36Z-5cc1ab76/project/vite.config.ts) to stop browser-test reload flakiness.
